subject: AP Computer Science Principles
theme: Computing innovations are best developed through collaboration, iterative design, and systematic debugging to create programs that serve a clear purpose.
core_concepts: A computing innovation can be physical (a robot), nonphysical (an app), or a combination of both — its purpose is WHY it exists while its function is WHAT it actually does.
Collaboration brings diverse perspectives and reduces errors — pair programming and code review are common collaborative practices tested on the exam.
Iterative development means repeatedly refining a program based on testing; incremental development means building and testing one feature at a time.
The four types of program errors are: syntax (grammar violation), runtime (crashes during execution), logic (runs but gives wrong results), and overflow (number too large to store).
Debugging is the process of finding and fixing errors — testing with specific inputs and tracing through code are key debugging strategies.
Documentation explains what a program does and how to use it; acknowledgment credits any code, libraries, or ideas borrowed from others.
Events are actions (clicks, keypresses) that trigger program responses — many modern programs are event-driven.

key_terms.definition: An innovation that uses a computing device to accomplish a task — can be physical, nonphysical, or both.
The reason a program was created — the problem it solves or goal it achieves.
What a program actually does when executed — its observable behaviors and actions.
A development process of repeatedly making small improvements and testing after each change.
A development process of adding and testing one feature at a time before building the next.
A collaboration practice where two developers share one workstation — one codes while the other reviews.
A systematic examination of source code by another person to find errors and improve quality.
A system that tracks changes to code over time, allowing programmers to recall previous versions.
An error caused by violating the grammar rules of the programming language — program will not run.
An error that occurs during program execution, causing the program to crash.
An error where the program runs without crashing but produces incorrect results due to flawed reasoning.
An error that occurs when a calculated value is too large to fit in the allocated memory space.
The process of identifying and correcting errors in a program.
Written explanation accompanying a program that describes its purpose, design, or usage.

exam_tip: The AP exam frequently asks you to distinguish program purpose (why) from program function (what), and to classify errors by type — know all four error types and which can be detected before vs. during runtime.
